title: S7comm CPU State Change Detection
app: TA for Zeek (Splunkbase 5466), Corelight App for Splunk (Splunkbase 3884)
spl:
index=ot sourcetype="zeek:s7comm:json"
| where match(subfunction_name,"(?i)STOP|START|RESUME") OR match(rosctr_name,"(?i)STOP|RUN|HOLD") OR match(function_name,"(?i)PLC|mode|cpu")
| stats count by source_h destination_h rosctr_name subfunction_name function_name
| sort - count
| head 100
description: CPU stop/start or mode transitions can halt a line or leave a PLC in an unsafe state; detecting them from the wire supports both troubleshooting and detection of malicious or accidental operational disruption.
value: CPU stop/start or mode transitions can halt a line or leave a PLC in an unsafe state; detecting them from the wire supports both troubleshooting and detection of malicious or accidental operational disruption.
implementation: Place Zeek on taps facing S7 controllers and HMIs; normalize `subfunction_name`/`rosctr_name` strings from production captures. Alert on stop/start patterns outside approved maintenance; correlate with MES/SCADA alarms for the same asset.
visualization: Timeline (state-related messages), Bar chart (count by subfunction_name), Table (source, destination, fields).
knownFalsePositives: CPU and run-state changes from routine mode switches, restarts, or process upsets with documented causes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: TA for Zeek (Splunkbase 5466), Corelight App for Splunk (Splunkbase 3884).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="zeek:s7comm:json"` (`rosctr_name`, `subfunction_name`, `function_name`, `error_class`).
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Place Zeek on taps facing S7 controllers and HMIs; normalize `subfunction_name`/`rosctr_name` strings from production captures. Alert on stop/start patterns outside approved maintenance; correlate with MES/SCADA alarms for the same asset.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="zeek:s7comm:json"
| where match(subfunction_name,"(?i)STOP|START|RESUME") OR match(rosctr_name,"(?i)STOP|RUN|HOLD") OR match(function_name,"(?i)PLC|mode|cpu")
| stats count by source_h destination_h rosctr_name subfunction_name function_name
| sort - count
| head 100
```

Understanding this SPL

**S7comm CPU State Change Detection** ‚Äî CPU stop/start or mode transitions can halt a line or leave a PLC in an unsafe state; detecting them from the wire supports both troubleshooting and detection of malicious or accidental operational disruption.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="zeek:s7comm:json"` (`rosctr_name`, `subfunction_name`, `function_name`, `error_class`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): TA for Zeek (Splunkbase 5466), Corelight App for Splunk (Splunkbase 3884). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: zeek:s7comm:json. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="zeek:s7comm:json". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where match(subfunction_name,"(?i)STOP|START|RESUME") OR match(rosctr_name,"(?i)STOP|RUN|HOLD") OR match(function_name,"(?i‚Ä¶` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by source_h destination_h rosctr_name subfunction_name function_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
‚Ä¢ Limits the number of rows with `head`.

Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Run the search for a short window and compare key fields with Zeek or Corelight protocol logs on the sensor for the same flow. Reconcile function codes, endpoints, and time order against a small known-good capture if needed.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timeline (state-related messages), Bar chart (count by subfunction_name), Table (source, destination, fields).
grandmaExplanation: We use deep packet inspection to see run-state and mode changes on our controllers so we can separate routine stops from something odd.
